text: The corrugated cardboard interior, that hidden honeycomb geography pressed between two smooth skins of brown paper, strikes me as one of the more quietly miraculous textures in ordinary life. You encounter it only in moments of destruction, when a box tears open along some unintended seam and reveals the fluted channels running parallel like a tiny mountain range, each ridge a perfect arch, the whole structure an argument that strength can be built from empty space rather than solid matter. There is something philosophically generous about this discovery: that what protects, what bears weight, what keeps fragile things intact through jostling transit and uncertain journeys, is itself mostly air held in elegant relation. Running a finger along those ridges produces a gentle percussion, a xylophone of impermanence, because the cardboard will be recycled or discarded once its purpose is spent. Perhaps this is what makes the texture oddly delightful rather than merely interesting: it belongs to the category of functional beauty, beauty that never asked to be noticed, that serves without ornament, that is most itself when being used up. The corrugations exist not for the eye but for the cargo, and we stumble upon their loveliness only in the act of undoing.
